content:
# Luke Chapter 9 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 97-99 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_009_page_001.jpg, LUK_009_page_002.jpg, LUK_009_page_003.jpg

---

**1** Jesus called the Twelve together and gave them power and authority over all demons, and the ability to heal diseases. **2** Then he sent them out to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal the sick. **3** He told them, "Take nothing for the journey — no walking stick, no travel bag, no bread, no money. Do not even bring an extra shirt. **4** Whatever house you enter, stay there until you leave that town. **5** If people do not welcome you, leave that town and shake the dust off your feet as a witness against them." **6** So they set out and traveled from village to village, announcing the good news and healing people everywhere.

**7** Now Herod the tetrarch heard about everything that was happening, and he was deeply troubled. Some were saying that John had been raised from the dead, **8** others that Elijah had appeared, and still others that one of the ancient prophets had come back to life. **9** Herod said, "I had John beheaded. So who is this I keep hearing about?" And he kept trying to see him.

**10** When the apostles returned, they reported to Jesus everything they had done. Then he took them with him and withdrew privately to a town called Bethsaida. **11** But the crowds found out and followed him. He welcomed them and spoke to them about the kingdom of God, and healed those who needed healing.

**12** As the day began to fade, the Twelve came to him and said, "Send the crowd away so they can go to the surrounding villages and countryside to find food and lodging, because we are in a remote place here."

**13** But he said to them, "You give them something to eat."

They replied, "All we have is five loaves of bread and two fish — unless we go and buy food for all these people." **14** (There were about five thousand men.)

He said to his disciples, "Have them sit down in groups of about fifty each." **15** They did so, and everyone sat down. **16** Then Jesus took the five loaves and the two fish, looked up to heaven, blessed them, broke them, and kept giving them to the disciples to distribute to the crowd. **17** Everyone ate and was satisfied. Afterward, the disciples picked up twelve baskets of leftover pieces.

**18** Once, when Jesus was praying in private and his disciples were with him, he asked them, "Who do the crowds say I am?"

**19** They answered, "Some say John the Baptist; others say Elijah; and still others say that one of the ancient prophets has come back to life."

**20** "But what about you?" he asked. "Who do you say I am?"

Peter answered, "The Christ of God."

**21** Jesus strictly warned them not to tell this to anyone. **22** He said, "The Son of Man must suffer many things. He will be rejected by the elders, chief priests, and scribes. He will be killed — and on the third day he will be raised."

**23** Then he said to everyone, "If anyone wants to follow me, they must deny themselves, take up their cross each day, and follow me. **24** For whoever wants to save their life will lose it, but whoever loses their life for my sake will save it. **25** What good does it do someone to gain the whole world and yet lose or forfeit their very self? **26** If anyone is ashamed of me and my words, the Son of Man will be ashamed of them when he comes in his glory and in the glory of the Father and of the holy angels. **27** I tell you the truth: some standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God."

**28** About eight days after he said these things, he took Peter, John, and James and went up on a mountain to pray. **29** As he was praying, the appearance of his face changed, and his clothing became dazzling white, blazing like lightning. **30** Suddenly, two men were talking with him — Moses and Elijah. **31** They appeared in glory and were speaking about his departure, which he was about to accomplish in Jerusalem.

**32** Peter and those with him were weighed down with sleep. But when they became fully awake, they saw his glory and the two men standing with him. **33** As Moses and Elijah were leaving, Peter said to Jesus, "Master, it is good that we are here. Let us put up three shelters — one for you, one for Moses, and one for Elijah." He did not really know what he was saying.

**34** While he was still speaking, a cloud came and overshadowed them, and they were afraid as they entered the cloud. **35** Then a voice came from the cloud, saying, "This is my Son, the Chosen One. Listen to him!" **36** When the voice had spoken, Jesus was found alone. The disciples kept this to themselves and told no one at that time anything of what they had seen.

**37** The next day, when they came down from the mountain, a large crowd met him. **38** A man in the crowd called out, "Teacher, I beg you to look at my son — he is my only child! **39** A spirit seizes him and he suddenly screams. It throws him into convulsions with foaming at the mouth. It mauls him and will hardly leave him alone. **40** I begged your disciples to drive it out, but they could not."

**41** Jesus responded, "You unbelieving and perverse generation! How long must I be with you and put up with you? Bring your son here." **42** Even as the boy was coming, the demon threw him to the ground in a convulsion. But Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, healed the boy, and gave him back to his father. **43** Everyone was amazed at the greatness of God.

While everyone was marveling at all that Jesus was doing, he said to his disciples, **44** "Let these words sink deep into your ears: the Son of Man is about to be handed over into the power of men." **45** But they did not understand what he meant. It was hidden from them so that they could not grasp it, and they were afraid to ask him about it.

**46** An argument arose among the disciples about which of them was the greatest. **47** Jesus, knowing the thoughts of their hearts, took a little child and had the child stand beside him. **48** Then he said to them, "Whoever welcomes this little child in my name welcomes me, and whoever welcomes me welcomes the one who sent me. For the one who is least among all of you — that is the one who is truly great."

**49** John spoke up and said, "Master, we saw someone driving out demons in your name, and we tried to stop him because he is not one of us."

**50** "Do not stop him," Jesus said, "for whoever is not against you is for you."

**51** As the time drew near for him to be taken up, Jesus resolutely set his face toward Jerusalem. **52** He sent messengers ahead of him. They went and entered a Samaritan village to make preparations for him, **53** but the people there would not welcome him, because he was heading toward Jerusalem. **54** When his disciples James and John saw this, they said, "Lord, do you want us to call fire down from heaven to destroy them?"

**55** But he turned and rebuked them. **56** And they went on to another village.

**57** As they were walking along the road, someone said to him, "I will follow you wherever you go."

**58** Jesus replied, "Foxes have dens, and birds of the sky have nests, but the Son of Man has no place to lay his head."

**59** He said to another person, "Follow me."

But the man replied, "Lord, first let me go and bury my father."

**60** Jesus said to him, "Let the dead bury their own dead. You go and proclaim the kingdom of God."

**61** Still another said, "I will follow you, Lord, but first let me go and say goodbye to those in my household."

**62** Jesus said to him, "No one who puts their hand to the plow and then looks back is fit for the kingdom of God."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **9:3 — "Take nothing for the journey"**: The Greek lists specific items — staff (ΡΑΒΔΟΝ), bag (ΠΗΡΑΝ), bread (ΑΡΤΟΝ), silver (ΑΡΓΥΡΙΟΝ), extra tunic (ΧΙΤΩΝΑΣ). This radical poverty was not asceticism for its own sake but a deliberate demonstration of trust in God's provision and dependence on the hospitality of those they served. The missionaries would be sustained by the very communities receiving the message.

- **9:5 — "Shake the dust off your feet"**: A symbolic Jewish act performed when leaving Gentile territory — treating an Israelite town as pagan ground. It declared the rejecting community responsible for its own response and released the messenger from further obligation.

- **9:9 — "He kept trying to see him"**: Sinaiticus uses ΕΖΗΤΕΙ ΙΔΕΙΝ ΑΥΤΟΝ, an imperfect tense indicating Herod's ongoing, persistent curiosity. This is not idle interest — it is the same Herod who beheaded John, and his desire to "see" Jesus carries ominous undertones that will resurface in Luke 23:8 during the trial.

- **9:13 — "You give them something to eat"**: The emphatic "you" (ΥΜΕΙΣ) places the responsibility directly on the disciples. Jesus does not simply perform miracles for spectators — he draws his followers into the work, teaching them that God's provision often flows through human hands.

- **9:17 — "Twelve baskets of leftover pieces"**: The number twelve corresponds to the twelve tribes of Israel. The abundance exceeds the need — God's provision is not merely sufficient but overflowing. Each apostle carries away a full basket, a tangible lesson in divine generosity.

- **9:20 — "The Christ of God"**: Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΝ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ. Peter's confession is strikingly brief compared to Matthew's version. Luke's Sinaiticus text has no "Son of the living God" — just the raw, essential claim: you are the Anointed One who belongs to God. The simplicity makes it all the more powerful.

- **9:22 — "must suffer many things"**: The word ΔΕΙ (dei, "must") signals divine necessity, not mere prediction. Jesus is not saying suffering is likely — he is saying it is the very path God has ordained. This "divine must" is a hallmark of Luke's theology: God's plan includes a suffering Messiah.

- **9:23 — "take up their cross each day"**: The phrase ΚΑΘΗΜΕΡΑΝ ("each day") is distinctive to Luke's account. Where Mark and Matthew present cross-bearing as a single dramatic commitment, Luke domesticates it into daily practice — a rhythm of self-denial woven into ordinary life.

- **9:27 — "will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God"**: This saying finds its immediate literary fulfillment in the Transfiguration that follows just eight days later (v. 28). Peter, John, and James witness the kingdom of God made visible in the glorified Jesus.

- **9:29 — "his clothing became dazzling white, blazing like lightning"**: Sinaiticus uses ΕΞΑΣΤΡΑΠΤΩΝ (exastrapton), a word for the flash of lightning. Luke avoids Mark's comparison to a bleacher's work; instead the imagery is entirely celestial — this is heavenly radiance, not laundered fabric.

- **9:31 — "his departure, which he was about to accomplish in Jerusalem"**: The Greek word is ΕΞΟΔΟΝ (exodon) — literally "exodus." Luke deliberately evokes Israel's foundational liberation story. Jesus' death in Jerusalem is not a tragedy to be endured but a new exodus to be "accomplished" — a liberation event that surpasses the original.

- **9:33 — "He did not really know what he was saying"**: Luke offers this editorial aside (ΜΗ ΕΙΔΩΣ Ο ΛΕΓΕΙ) to soften Peter's blunder. Peter's instinct to build shelters tries to make permanent what is meant to be a momentary revelation, and to place Jesus on equal footing with Moses and Elijah — an arrangement the heavenly voice immediately corrects.

- **9:35 — "my Son, the Chosen One"**: Sinaiticus reads Ο ΕΚΛΕΛΕΓΜΕΝΟΣ ("the Chosen One") rather than the more common Ο ΑΓΑΠΗΤΟΣ ("the Beloved") found in later manuscripts. This is one of the most significant textual variants in Luke. "Chosen One" echoes Isaiah 42:1 and the Servant Songs, directly identifying Jesus as God's chosen Servant who will suffer for the people.

- **9:45 — "It was hidden from them"**: The passive construction (ΗΝ ΠΑΡΑΚΕΚΑΛΥΜΜΕΝΟΝ) implies divine concealment. The disciples' incomprehension is not merely intellectual failure — there is a veil over their understanding that will not be lifted until after the resurrection (Luke 24:31, 45). God controls the timing of revelation.

- **9:51 — "resolutely set his face toward Jerusalem"**: The phrase ΤΟ ΠΡΟΣΩΠΟΝ ΕΣΤΗΡΙΣΕΝ (to prosopon esterisen) echoes the Hebrew prophets (Isaiah 50:7) and signals absolute determination. This verse marks the great turning point of Luke's Gospel — the beginning of the lengthy "Travel Narrative" (9:51-19:27) in which everything moves purposefully toward Jerusalem and the cross. Jesus does not drift toward his death; he marches.

- **9:53 — "because he was heading toward Jerusalem"**: The Samaritan rejection is theological, not personal. Samaritans and Jews disputed whether God should be worshipped on Mount Gerizim or in Jerusalem. Jesus' deliberate journey toward Jerusalem embodied a claim the Samaritans could not accept.

- **9:54 — "call fire down from heaven"**: James and John echo the prophet Elijah's action in 2 Kings 1:10-12. Their instinct is to use divine power for retribution, but Jesus' rebuke signals a fundamentally different mission. The kingdom of God does not advance through the destruction of those who reject it.

- **9:60 — "Let the dead bury their own dead"**: One of Jesus' most jarring sayings. Burying a parent was among the most sacred duties in Jewish culture — to neglect it was virtually unthinkable. Jesus uses deliberate shock to establish that the call of the kingdom creates obligations that supersede even the most deeply honored human bonds. The "dead" who bury are the spiritually dead — those not yet alive to God's call.

- **9:62 — "puts their hand to the plow and then looks back"**: A farmer who plows while looking backward will create crooked furrows and ruin the field. The image is practical and vivid: discipleship requires undivided forward attention. Elisha in 1 Kings 19:19-21 was allowed to say goodbye before following Elijah, but the new exodus demands even greater urgency. The kingdom of God will not wait.
